principles.label: Synthetic A Priori Knowledge
principles.summary: Human understanding requires a priori categories to form objective concepts and make universal, necessary judgments about the world.
principles.details: Universal and necessary knowledge cannot be derived purely from particular experiences (a posteriori), nor solely through logical analysis of concepts (analytic). It requires a priori cognition contributed by our own cognitive faculties.
principles.representative_quote: The conditions necessary for the knowledge of an object require a priori categories as the enabling conditions of all human understanding.

principles.label: Space and Time as Forms of Intuition
principles.summary: Space and time are pure forms of human sensibility, not self-subsisting entities or properties of things in themselves.
principles.details: This explains how synthetic a priori propositions, like those in geometry and mathematics, can be necessarily true of the experienced world. We structure experience by contributing space and time to it.
principles.representative_quote: space and time are neither subsistent beings nor inherent in things as they are in themselves, but are rather only forms of our sensibility

principles.label: Phenomena vs. Noumena
principles.summary: We can only have knowledge of things as they appear to us (phenomena), never as they are in themselves (noumena).
principles.details: The categories of understanding only yield valid cognition when applied to sensible experience. Attempting to apply them beyond the bounds of sense leads to illusion.
principles.representative_quote: we cognize these things not as they are in themselves but only as they appear under the conditions of our sensibility

principles.label: The Categorical Imperative (Universalizability)
principles.summary: Moral actions must be based on universalizable maxims that you could rationally will everyone else to follow.
principles.details: If a principle of action cannot be adopted by everyone without undermining the concept it relies upon, acting on it means you are unfairly exempting yourself from the rules that govern all rational beings.
principles.representative_quote: I ought never to act except in such a way that I could also will that my maxim should become a universal law

principles.label: Persons as Ends in Themselves
principles.summary: Morality is grounded in our nature as rational persons; humans must be treated as ends in themselves, never merely as means.
principles.details: Persons possess inherent rationality and dignity. Using them merely as tools or objects to achieve a goal, even a seemingly good one, violates their fundamental moral status.
principles.representative_quote: persons are, essentially, rational creatures who are deserving of respect. This rationality grounds what Kant calls The Categorical Imperative

principles.label: Public vs. Private Use of Reason
principles.summary: The public use of reason must always be free to ensure enlightenment, while the private use in civic duties may be restricted.
principles.details: As scholars addressing the public, individuals must have absolute freedom to criticize and propose reforms. However, in their civic roles (e.g., as soldiers or civil servants), they must obey orders to maintain societal function.
principles.representative_quote: the public use of one's reason must be free at all times, and this alone can bring enlightenment to mankind.

principles.label: Republicanism and Perpetual Peace
principles.summary: Lasting global peace requires states to have republican constitutions and to form a federation of free states.
principles.details: Representative governments with a separation of powers naturally trend toward pacifism. A league of such independent states solves international anarchy without risking the tyranny of a single world government.
principles.representative_quote: The civil constitution of each state shall be republican.

principles.label: The Absolute Prohibition of Lying
principles.summary: A lie, even if seemingly harmless, is a serious violation of a duty to oneself and subverts human dignity.
principles.details: Lying attacks the roots of human thinking and destroys the mutual esteem for character that is necessary for true friendship and love.
principles.representative_quote: It is a serious violation of a duty to oneself; it subverts the dignity of humanity in our own person, and attacks the roots of our thinking.

principles.label: Grounding Thought in Sensible Intuition
principles.summary: All thought and pure understanding must ultimately be grounded in sensible intuition to yield valid cognition.
principles.details: Understanding cannot generate knowledge on its own; it requires the receptivity of sensibility because there is no other way for objects to be given to us.
principles.representative_quote: all thought … must ultimately be related to intuition, thus, in our case, to sensibility, since there is no other way in which the objects can be given to us

principles.label: Existence is Not a Predicate
principles.summary: Existence does not add an objective attribute to a subject's essence; it is an absolute position.
principles.details: This principle refutes ontological arguments that attempt to prove God's existence purely from logical definitions. Existence merely posits the object as available to perception or thought.
principles.representative_quote: When the verb 'is' is used not as a copula to relate a subject to its predicate... but is asserted abruptly, absolutely... it signifies, Kant claims, a mere positing of an object.

frameworks.label: Critique of Reason / Transcendental Deduction
frameworks.summary: A method for determining the valid scope of human knowledge by examining the limits of our cognitive powers.
frameworks.details: 1. Acknowledge that all experience must be ascribed to a single identical subject. 2. Identify the a priori forms of intuition (space and time) and categories of understanding. 3. Restrict the application of these a priori concepts strictly to the phenomenal world (appearances), denying their application to things in themselves.
frameworks.representative_quote: 'Critique,' in Kant's sense of the term, consists in examining the scope and limits of our cognitive powers... in order to decide to what extent, if any, metaphysics is possible for us human beings.

frameworks.label: The Categorical Imperative Test
frameworks.summary: A decision framework for determining whether a proposed action or personal rule is morally permissible.
frameworks.details: 1. Identify your maxim (the subjective principle behind your action). 2. Imagine if that maxim were a universal law that everyone followed. 3. Determine if universalizing it would create a logical contradiction that undoes the action's premise. 4. If it cannot be willed as a universal law, the action is morally wrong.
frameworks.representative_quote: Act only in accordance with that maxim through which you can at the same time will that it become a universal law.

frameworks.label: Public vs. Private Use of Reason
frameworks.summary: A framework for balancing civic obedience with intellectual freedom.
frameworks.details: 1. Identify the context: are you acting in a civic post (private use) or as a scholar addressing the public (public use)? 2. If in a civic post, remain passive and obey orders to ensure institutional function. 3. If acting as a scholar, exercise unlimited freedom to publish criticisms and propose improvements.
frameworks.representative_quote: By "public use of one's reason" I mean that use which a man, as scholar , makes of it before the reading public. I call "private use" that use which a man makes of his reason in a civic post

frameworks.label: The Perpetual Peace Program
frameworks.summary: A structured political program for governments to transition from international hostility to permanent peace.
frameworks.details: 1. Implement Preliminary Articles (abolish secret treaties, standing armies, and national debts for war). 2. Establish Definitive Articles: ensure all states have republican constitutions. 3. Form a federation of these free states. 4. Guarantee universal hospitality for global citizens.
frameworks.representative_quote: Kant proposed a peace program to be implemented by governments. The "Preliminary Articles" described these steps that should be taken immediately... Three Definitive Articles would provide not merely a cessation of hostilities but a foundation on which to build a peace.

frameworks.label: Calculus of Real-Opposition
frameworks.summary: A method for analyzing conflicting natural forces or psychological states to determine their net effect without assuming logical contradiction.
frameworks.details: 1. Identify two opposing forces as inherently positive magnitudes of the same kind (e.g., pleasure and aversion). 2. Assign one as positive and the contrary as negative relative to it. 3. Sum the magnitudes to find the net effect. 4. Recognize that a net zero result does not annihilate the subject bearing these modifications.
frameworks.representative_quote: In all natural changes of the world their positive sum, insofar as it is estimated by the addition of agreeing (not opposed) positions and the subtraction from one another of those in real-opposition, is neither increased nor diminished.

mental_models.label: Copernican Revolution in Philosophy
mental_models.summary: A paradigm shift that re-centers understanding around the subject (the observer) rather than the object (the observed).
mental_models.details: Just as Copernicus shifted the center of the solar system to the sun, Kant shifted philosophy by arguing that objects must conform to our cognition, rather than our cognition passively conforming to objects.
mental_models.representative_quote: The philosopher Immanuel Kant discussed a philosophical version of the Copernican Revolution in which the priority shifted from the object to the subject...

mental_models.label: Phenomena vs. Things in Themselves (Noumena)
mental_models.summary: The distinction between the world as it appears to us (structured by our minds) and the world as it exists independently of our perception.
mental_models.details: This model prevents the overreach of reason by reminding us that our scientific and empirical knowledge applies only to the phenomenal realm.
mental_models.representative_quote: our sense representation is not a representation of things in themselves, but of the way in which they appear to us.

mental_models.label: Persons as Ends in Themselves
mental_models.summary: Viewing human beings not as tools or stepping stones, but as entities with inherent value and rationality that demand respect.
mental_models.details: This lens fundamentally alters ethical calculations, rejecting the idea that a person can be sacrificed or manipulated for the 'greater good'.
mental_models.representative_quote: Act in such a way that you treat humanity, whether in your own person or in the person of any other, never merely as a means to an end, but always at the same time as an end.

mental_models.label: Guardians and Minors
mental_models.summary: A lens for viewing societal control where the majority willingly remain 'minors' due to laziness, allowing a few 'guardians' to dictate their thoughts.
mental_models.details: Guardians maintain power by exaggerating the dangers of independent thought, treating the public like domestic cattle and preventing enlightenment.
mental_models.representative_quote: Laziness and cowardice are the reasons why such a large part of mankind gladly remain minors all their lives, long after nature has freed them from external guidance.

mental_models.label: Federation vs. World Government
mental_models.summary: Balancing global order and sovereignty by favoring a voluntary league of independent republics over a monolithic world state.
mental_models.details: A single world government trends toward tyranny and oppression. Order is better achieved through a confederation of free states bound by mutual agreements.
mental_models.representative_quote: The law of nations shall be founded on a federation of free states.

mental_models.label: Flight in Empty Space
mental_models.summary: A metaphor for pure understanding attempting to operate without the grounding of sensible experience.
mental_models.details: Just as a bird needs the resistance of air to fly, human understanding needs the 'resistance' of sensible intuition. Without it, reason merely spins illusions.
mental_models.representative_quote: Plato abandoned the world of the senses because it posed so many hindrances for the understanding, and dared to go beyond it on the wings of the ideas, in the empty space of pure understanding.

mental_models.label: Real-Opposition
mental_models.summary: Viewing negations in nature and psychology not as mere absences, but as positive forces acting in contrary directions.
mental_models.details: For example, aversion is not just the absence of pleasure, but a 'negative pleasure'—a positive psychological force acting against natural inclination.
mental_models.representative_quote: Real-repugnance takes place between two positives, power against power, which cannot be contradictories but must be of the same kind

heuristics.label: Sapere aude! (Dare to know)
heuristics.summary: Have the courage to use your own understanding without the guidance of another.
heuristics.representative_quote: This nonage is self-imposed if its cause lies not in lack of understanding but in indecision and lack of courage to use one's own mind without another's guidance.

heuristics.label: Argue, but obey
heuristics.summary: Argue as much as you please about what you please in public discourse, but strictly obey the rules of your civic duties.
heuristics.representative_quote: Argue as much as you like, and about what you like, but obey!

heuristics.label: Never treat humanity merely as a means
heuristics.summary: Always interact with others recognizing their inherent dignity and rationality, never just as tools for your own ends.
heuristics.representative_quote: Act in such a way that you treat humanity, whether in your own person or in the person of any other, never merely as a means to an end, but always at the same time as an end.

heuristics.label: Universalize your maxims
heuristics.summary: Before acting, ask if you could rationally will that the principle behind your action becomes a universal law for everyone.
heuristics.representative_quote: I ought never to act except in such a way that I could also will that my maxim should become a universal law

heuristics.label: Distinguish reticence from dishonesty
heuristics.summary: It is permissible to withhold the whole truth (reticence), but it is never permissible to say what you know to be false (dishonesty).
heuristics.representative_quote: What the honest but reticent man says is true, but not the whole truth. What the dishonest man says is something he knows to be false.

signature_quotes.label: The Starry Heavens and the Moral Law
signature_quotes.summary: Kant's summary of his dual philosophical goals: explaining the certainty of science and the possibility of human freedom.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: Two things fill the mind with ever new and increasing admiration and awe the more often and more enduringly reflection is occupied with them: the starry heavens above me and the moral law within me.

signature_quotes.label: Definition of Enlightenment
signature_quotes.summary: The opening thesis of his essay on enlightenment.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: Enlightenment is man's emergence from his self-imposed nonage.

signature_quotes.label: The Categorical Imperative (First Formulation)
signature_quotes.summary: The core test for moral permissibility.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: Act only in accordance with that maxim through which you can at the same time will that it become a universal law.

signature_quotes.label: The Categorical Imperative (Second Formulation)
signature_quotes.summary: The requirement to respect the inherent dignity of rational beings.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: Act in such a way that you treat humanity, whether in your own person or in the person of any other, never merely as a means to an end, but always at the same time as an end.

signature_quotes.label: Limits of Sense Representation
signature_quotes.summary: A concise statement of transcendental idealism.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: our sense representation is not a representation of things in themselves, but of the way in which they appear to us.

signature_quotes.label: Laziness and Cowardice
signature_quotes.summary: Explaining why people fail to think for themselves.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: Laziness and cowardice are the reasons why such a large part of mankind gladly remain minors all their lives, long after nature has freed them from external guidance.

signature_quotes.label: Republican Constitution
signature_quotes.summary: The first Definitive Article for building a foundation of peace.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: The civil constitution of each state shall be republican.

signature_quotes.label: Impossibility of Nothingness
signature_quotes.summary: Arguing that possibility depends on actuality.
signature_quotes.representative_quote: It is absolutely impossible that nothing should exist.

anti_patterns.label: Dogmatic Metaphysics
anti_patterns.summary: Attempting to acquire knowledge of things as they are in themselves (like the soul or God) using pure reason alone.
anti_patterns.details: The categories of understanding only yield valid cognition when applied to spatiotemporal data. Inferences of pure reason performed without respect to the limits of sensibility give rise only to metaphysical illusion.
anti_patterns.representative_quote: Without such prior critique of our cognitive powers, Kant maintains, either affirming or denying the possibility of metaphysics is sheer dogmatism

anti_patterns.label: Strict Empiricism
anti_patterns.summary: Grounding knowledge solely on ideas acquired in the course of individual experience.
anti_patterns.details: Empiricism fails because it cannot account for the universal and necessary validity (a priori knowledge) required for the fundamental principles of natural science and mathematics.
anti_patterns.representative_quote: The conditions necessary for the knowledge of an object require a priori categories as the enabling conditions of all human understanding.

anti_patterns.label: Consequentialism and Realpolitik
anti_patterns.summary: Evaluating the morality of actions solely based on their outcomes, or abandoning moral principles in politics because others might act immorally.
anti_patterns.details: This ignores the inherent rationality and value of persons, permitting actions that use individuals as mere objects or tools if it results in a better overall outcome.
anti_patterns.representative_quote: This regret is not motivated by anything moral, since it is produced by an awareness not of the act itself, but of its consequences.

anti_patterns.label: The 'Harmless' Lie
anti_patterns.summary: Telling a lie to preserve a relationship or prevent a tragedy.
anti_patterns.details: Lying always involves using someone as a mere means, bypassing their ability to consent. It destroys mutual esteem for character, which is the necessary foundation for true friendship.
anti_patterns.representative_quote: It is a serious violation of a duty to oneself; it subverts the dignity of humanity in our own person, and attacks the roots of our thinking.

anti_patterns.label: Committing to Unalterable Dogmas
anti_patterns.summary: Binding succeeding generations to unalterable doctrines by oath.
anti_patterns.details: This annihilates a period of time in the progress of human improvement, preventing future generations from purging errors. Kant calls this a crime against human nature.
anti_patterns.representative_quote: An epoch cannot conclude a pact that will commit succeeding ages, prevent them from increasing their significant insights, purging themselves of errors, and generally progressing in enlightenment. That would be a crime against human nature

anti_patterns.label: Single World Government
anti_patterns.summary: Establishing a single global government to enforce peace.
anti_patterns.details: While aiming to solve international anarchy, a monolithic world state would inevitably be prone to tyranny, replacing conflict with global oppression.
anti_patterns.representative_quote: The law of nations shall be founded on a federation of free states.
